---
title: "Qualifier 3: Support Students in Their Choice and Navigation of Formal and Informal Learning Pathways"
---

::: qualifier-badge
Qualifier 3 · Learning Pathways
:::

I actively support students in navigating both formal and informal learning pathways by making learning processes explicit and providing structured opportunities for choice, progression, and self-directed development. A central principle of my curriculum design is that students should not only develop disciplinary knowledge, but should develop the ability to navigate their own learning — into the subject, through the degree, and into professional practice — aligning with the principles of Self-Regulated Learning [@bjork2013]. 

------------------------------------------------------------------------

## Transition Into and Through the Curriculum

Many students arrive in BIOL340 from BIOL215 with strong laboratory foundations but limited experience in coding or data analysis. The early weeks bridge this gap through scaffolded practicals, curated resources, and flexible entry points designed to manage cognitive load @sweller1988] — introducing computational concepts incrementally so that new skills build on established disciplinary knowledge rather than competing with it. This scaffolded progression is embedded within a constructively aligned curriculum [@biggs2011], where learning activities, resources, and assessments are intentionally sequenced so that students can see a clear path from guided instruction to independent practice. I normalise this transition in my teaching language, framing new tools as an expected part of becoming a modern biologist. In lectures and dry lab discussions, I make connections across the degree explicit, highlighting how skills developed in BIOL215 and BIOL340 transfer into CHEM325 and honours research, helping students navigate their formal pathway as a coherent trajectory.

> *"They \[the BIOL340 wet and dry practicals\] worked very well together and had a good flow between them. They built knowledge from a more simple to a more complex level."* — BIOL340 student, 2025.

------------------------------------------------------------------------

## Student Choice and Informal Pathways

Within this structured framework, I create deliberate space for student agency. In the data visualisation components, students are provided with coding examples but encouraged to make their own decisions about how to present their data, using generative AI to explore alternative approaches that they then evaluate and justify. This aligns with UDL's emphasis on providing multiple means of action and expression [@cast2024UDL] — students engage with the same learning outcomes through pathways that reflect their developing confidence and creativity, rather than following a single prescribed route.

Beyond formal teaching, I have developed extension resources using Google NotebookLM, including podcasts and summary videos embedded in the Moodle site as informal learning tools. These provide multiple means of representation [@cast2024UDL] allowing students to revisit concepts in formats that suit their learning preferences. Students are also encouraged to create their own study guides using similar tools, further developing their capacity for self-directed learning. The staged assessment structure supports this transition to independence: moving from a scientific report to a generative AI reflection task requires students to evaluate their own learning processes, promoting metacognitive awareness and reducing the cognitive demands of tackling both scientific communication and reflective thinking simultaneously (Sweller, 1988).

![Example lecture summary video](images/example_refresher.mp4){fig-alt="Google notebookLM generated content refresher video" width="100%"} 


<figure>
  <audio controls style="width: 100%;">
    <source src="images/example_podcast.m4a" type="audio/mp4">
    Your browser does not support the audio element.
  </audio>
  <figcaption>Google NotebookLM generated lecture summary podcast</figcaption>
</figure>


------------------------------------------------------------------------

## Preparation for the Workforce

Digital literacy and by extension digital competence are essential skills in the post-Covid and post-Gen-AI eras [@morgan2022, @lucas2022, @Zhou2025]. My curriculum redesign frames computational and digital literacy as essential biology graduate skills. In dry lab discussions, I situate what students are learning within the context of employer and research expectations. Student feedback supports this framing: BIOL340 students scored a mean of 4.75/6.0 on feeling equipped to work in Cell and Molecular Biology, with qualitative responses noting that computational activities reflect current industry practice.

------------------------------------------------------------------------

**Evidence:** Student evaluation reports are available in the evidence folder. See also the [BIOL340 teaching website](https://nikolasjohnston.github.io/BIOL340_DP/pages/DP1).

------------------------------------------------------------------------

**Previous:** [Qualifier 2 — Inclusive Learning](cpd2.html) · **Next:** [Qualifier 4 — Scaffolded Assessment](cpd4.html)
